ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for lobster and beef rib pho. FOODON_00002700 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (noodle-based soup) is the closest parent. No specific pho or lobster-beef pho code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual prepared food dishes at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: fresh herbs (Thai basil, coriander, spring onion)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of lobster and beef rib pho weighing approximately 700 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, lobster pieces, beef ribs, and fresh herb garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Pho bo (Vietnamese beef noodle soup) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA lobster and beef rib component data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for lobster and beef rib pho exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by component-weighted assembly: standard pho broth with rice noodles (USDA FDC #174891 Vietnamese beef noodle soup analogue), lobster meat (USDA FDC #175180 lobster cooked), and beef short ribs (USDA FDC #174034 beef ribs braised). Weighted proportions per 100 g of assembled dish: broth+noodles ~55%, lobster ~20%, beef rib ~20%, herbs/garnish ~5%. Energy and macros computed from component contributions. This is a premium restaurant dish sold at SGD 40.90; nutrient density is higher than standard pho due to lobster and beef rib content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lobster and beef rib pho. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant glycemic driver being rice noodles (bánh phở). Rice noodles have a published GI of approximately 40–53 (mean ~45) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The substantial protein (lobster, beef rib) and fat content of this dish further attenuates the glycemic response relative to plain rice noodles. GI 45 is a conservative mid-range estimate for rice-noodle-based soups with significant protein and fat co-ingestion. gi_category set to 'low' per threshold ≤55. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 8.5 × 7.0) / 100 = 26.8 g (based on 700 g serving with 8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from pho broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100 g (approximately 3360 mg per 700 g bowl), which substantially exceeds the recommended 2300 mg daily sodium limit in a single serving; requesting reduced broth volume or avoiding drinking the broth can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by high protein and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a full 700 g bowl carries an estimated glycemic load of approximately 27 g; the high protein from lobster and beef ribs slows gastric emptying and attenuates the glycemic response, but portion control and post-meal blood glucose monitoring are advisable, particularly given the large serving size typical of restaurant pho.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef ribs and bone broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should exercise caution with this dish due to its high sodium load (~3360 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat contribution from beef ribs and bone broth; lobster provides heart-beneficial lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids, but the overall sodium burden makes frequent consumption inadvisable for those managing blood pressure or heart failure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lobster and Beef Rib Pho is a premium Vietnamese-style noodle soup dish served in Singapore, combining two luxury proteins — whole or halved lobster and slow-braised beef ribs — in a deeply aromatic pho broth. The broth is prepared by simmering beef bones for many hours with charred ginger, onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and fish sauce, producing a rich, clear, amber-coloured stock that forms the flavour backbone of the dish. Silky flat rice noodles (bánh phở) are placed in the bowl, topped with the lobster and tender fall-off-the-bone beef ribs, then ladled with the hot broth and finished with fresh bean sprouts, Thai basil, coriander, spring onion, lime wedges, and sliced chilli. The dish is sold at Viet'Spice on Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 40.90, positioning it firmly in the premium restaurant segment. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-dense (~9.2 g per 100 g) due to the lobster and beef rib content, with moderate fat (~3.8 g per 100 g) and a relatively low glycemic index (~45) driven by rice noodles but attenuated by the high protein and fat co-ingestion. The primary clinical concern is sodium: the broth-heavy preparation delivers an estimated 3360 mg sodium per 700 g bowl, making it a high-sodium meal relevant to hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Lobster contributes lean protein, vitamin B12, selenium, and zinc, while beef ribs add iron, collagen-derived amino acids, and saturated fat. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarian-adjacent diets, but is not halal-certified by default due to beef and the restaurant context. It is a culturally significant fusion of Vietnamese pho tradition with Singaporean premium dining culture.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for lobster and beef rib pho exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Values are estimated by component-weighted assembly from USDA FoodData Central entries for Vietnamese beef noodle soup, cooked lobster, and braised beef ribs. GI is analogously estimated from published rice noodle GI values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Vietnamese beef noodle soup (FDC #174891 analogue), cooked lobster (FDC #175180), and braised beef ribs (FDC #174034). No direct approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources for this dish type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources used for this estimation. Serving size of 700 g is estimated based on typical restaurant pho bowl weights for premium dishes. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Viet'Spice, Circular Road, Singapore) does not appear to carry halal certification based on available information; consumers requiring halal-certified food should verify directly with the restaurant. The food_group is retained as 'Fish and seafood' per the input record, reflecting the lobster as the primary premium protein; an argument could be made for 'Mixed' given the beef rib content. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_g) / 100 = (45 × 8.5 × 700) / 100 / 100 = 26.775 ≈ 26.8 g.
